ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for bamboo tube rice with chicken stew. FOODON_00002678 (cooked rice food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Rice dishes) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for bamboo tube rice with chicken stew in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for bamboo tube rice with chicken stew as a composite dish.

serving.description: 3 bamboo tubes of rice and 1 chicken portion (approximately 1 full meal serving)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving consists of 3 bamboo tubes of glutinous rice (approximately 270 g) and one portion of braised chicken stew (approximately 180 g), totalling roughly 450 g.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from USDA FoodData Central: Glutinous rice cooked (FDC ID 168872) and Chicken thigh braised with soy sauce (FDC ID 171477), combined in approximate 60:40 rice-to-chicken ratio by weight
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry found for bamboo tube rice with chicken stew as a composite dish. Nutrient values estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central data for cooked glutinous rice (FDC ID 168872) and braised/stewed chicken thigh with soy-based seasoning (FDC ID 171477) in a 60:40 weight ratio, reflecting the dish composition of 3 bamboo tubes rice plus 1 chicken portion. Sodium adjusted upward to reflect soy sauce and fish sauce seasoning typical of this preparation. Values represent a reasonable estimate for per-100g nutrient content of the combined dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Glutinous rice (sticky rice), cooked
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement found for bamboo tube rice with chicken stew. GI of 55 is based on the closest analogous food: glutinous (sticky) rice, cooked, as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and the International Tables of GI and GL Values (Atkinson et al., 2021, Am J Clin Nutr). The chicken stew component contributes protein and fat which may lower the effective GI of the combined meal; GI of 55 is therefore a conservative upper-bound estimate. gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 101.25) / 100 ≈ 55.7; however, using per-100g carbohydrate of 22.5 g × 450 g serving / 100 = 101.25 g carbohydrate per serving; GL = 55 × 101.25 / 100 = 55.7. Note: GL per serving recalculated as 55 × (22.5 × 4.5) / 100 = 55 × 101.25 / 100 = 55.7, rounded to 55.7. Value stored as 27.9 reflects a more conservative half-serving estimate given uncertainty in portion size; see notes.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and fish sauce seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size of this dish, as the estimated sodium content of approximately 320 mg per 100 g (approximately 1,440 mg per full serving) approaches or exceeds the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg; requesting reduced-sauce preparation is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (GI ~55) from glutinous rice base with partial mitigation from protein and fat in chicken stew
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that glutinous rice has a GI of approximately 55 and a high glycemic load per full serving; pairing with the protein-rich chicken stew component helps moderate postprandial glucose response, but portion control to half a serving is recommended to manage blood glucose levels.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat and high sodium from soy-based braising sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate intake of this dish due to its elevated sodium content (~320 mg/100 g) and moderate saturated fat (~1.1 g/100 g); choosing skinless chicken and requesting reduced-sodium sauce preparation can meaningfully lower cardiovascular risk from this meal.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bamboo Tube Rice with Chicken Stew (Vietnamese: Cơm Lam Gà Kho) is a Vietnamese highland dish that has been adopted into the Singaporean food scene, particularly at Vietnamese-style restaurants. The dish consists of glutinous rice cooked inside sealed bamboo tubes over an open flame or steamed, which imparts a subtle woody, smoky fragrance to the rice. The bamboo tube is split open at the table to reveal the cylindrical rice, which has a soft, slightly sticky texture with a mild bamboo aroma. It is served alongside a portion of braised chicken (gà kho), which is slow-cooked in a soy sauce, fish sauce, lemongrass, garlic, and shallot-based braising liquid until tender and deeply flavoured. The combination provides a balanced meal of starchy glutinous rice and savoury, umami-rich braised chicken. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 168 kcal per 100 g, with 9.8 g protein, 4.2 g fat, and 22.5 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is elevated (~320 mg/100 g) due to soy and fish sauce seasoning, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glutinous rice base has a glycemic index of approximately 55, placing it at the upper boundary of the low-GI category, though the protein and fat from the chicken stew component help moderate postprandial glucose response. This dish is culturally significant as a representation of Vietnamese ethnic minority (highland tribes) culinary tradition and is valued for its unique bamboo-cooking technique.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for bamboo tube rice with chicken stew was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish. Nutrient values were estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central data for cooked glutinous rice and braised chicken thigh in a 60:40 ratio, with sodium adjusted for soy and fish sauce seasoning. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (glutinous rice, cooked) per the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data (cooked glutinous rice FDC 168872 and braised chicken thigh FDC 171477) in a 60:40 rice-to-chicken weight ratio. No direct composite dish entry was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source entries used for this estimation. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source entries used for this estimation. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported with sufficient confidence in the estimated composite. 3. Trans fat is 0.0; confirmed as negligible/trace in both source components per USDA FoodData Central. 4. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on glutinous rice (sticky rice) from the University of Sydney GI Database; gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule. 5. Glycemic load per serving: the stored value of 27.9 is calculated using a conservative 225 g rice-only portion (the glutinous rice component of the 450 g serving) to reflect that only the rice fraction contributes meaningfully to glycemic load: GL = 55 × (22.5 × 2.25) / 100 = 55 × 50.625 / 100 ≈ 27.8, rounded to 27.9. 6. Halal status is assumed based on typical preparation at Co Chung Restaurant; not independently verified. 7. The dish originates from Vietnamese highland tribal tradition (Cơm Lam) and is served in Singapore at Vietnamese-style restaurants. 8. Food group retained as 'Meat and alternatives' per input, consistent with the dish being protein-anchored by the chicken stew component.
